questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Parts of the following sentence are given as options. Identify the part that contains a grammatical error. If there is no error, select 'No error'. Such was the education / of the Spartans with regard to / one of the greatest of there kings.
options: one of the greatest of there kings.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} of the Spartans with regard to
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The part that contains a grammatical error is: "one of the greatest of there kings." The word "there" is used to refer to a place or location (e.g., "over there"). In this context, the correct word should be "their," which is a possessive pronoun indicating that something belongs to them. Corrected sentence: "Such was the education of the Spartans with regard to one of the greatest of their kings."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the word that can substitute the given group of words. Belonging to the same kind
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The word that can substitute the given group of words "Belonging to the same kind" is: Homogeneous Homogeneous: Of the same kind or nature; consisting of parts or elements that are all of the same kind. Heterogeneous: Composed of different kinds or types; diverse in character or content. Casserole: A dish, usually of meat, vegetables, and sometimes pasta, that is cooked slowly in an oven. Sebaceous: Relating to oil or fat; specifically, relating to the sebaceous glands or their secretions.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the correctly spelt word.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correctly spelt word is: MALODOROUS Malodorous: Having a bad odor; smelling very unpleasant. Pestilential: Relating to or tending to cause infectious diseases; harmful or destructive, especially to crops or livestock. Hierarchy: A system in which people or things are arranged according to their importance or rank. Appalling: Causing shock or dismay; horrific.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the word that is closest in meaning (SYNONYM) to the word given below. BOGUS
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The word that is closest in meaning (synonym) to "BOGUS" is: FALSE Bogus: Not genuine or true; fake. Humane: Having or showing compassion or benevolence. False: Not true or correct; intended to deceive. Strong: Having great physical power, energy, or strength; not easily broken or damaged. Criticize: To express disapproval of someone or something; to point out the faults of someone or something.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Identify the segment in the sentence which contains a grammatical error. If there is no error, then select the option "No error". And, indeed, there was/ hardly picture in Rome I was unable/ to reattribute to its rightful owner.
options: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} to reattribute to its rightful owner.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} hardly picture in Rome I was unable
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The segment in the sentence which contains a grammatical error is: hardly picture in Rome I was unable The phrase "hardly picture" is missing an article. It should be "hardly a picture" to be grammatically correct. The corrected sentence is: "And, indeed, there was hardly a picture in Rome I was unable to reattribute to its rightful owner."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Improve the underlined part of the sentence. Choose ‘No improvement’ as an answer if the sentence is grammatically correct. His shoes were s̲o̲a̲k̲e̲d̲ ̲f̲r̲o̲m̲ ̲w̲a̲l̲k̲ through the dew.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct improvement for the underlined part of the sentence is: Soaked from walking The corrected phrase "soaked from walking" uses the gerund form "walking," which functions as a noun and indicates the action of walking. This form is appropriate because it expresses the activity that caused the shoes to become soaked.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the option that is the correct passive form of the sentence. We had eaten the cake.
options: The cake is eaten by us.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The cake had been eaten by us.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The cake was eaten by us.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The cake will be eaten by us.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct passive form of the sentence "We had eaten the cake." is: The cake had been eaten by us. Active voice ---- Passive voice Subject + had + V3 + Object ---- Object + had + been + V3 + by + subject Therefore, the correct answer is: “The cake had been eaten by us.”

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the word that is opposite in meaning (ANTONYM) to the word given below Polarize
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is a: Combine Polarize: To divide or cause to divide into sharply contrasting groups or factions, often with opposing views or beliefs. Segregate: To separate or set apart from others or from the main group, especially on the basis of race, religion, or gender. Combine: To unite or bring together separate elements or entities into one. Orchestrate: To arrange or direct the elements of a situation, event, or process in a skillful or coordinated manner. Conjoin: To join or merge together, especially in a close or intimate manner.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the option that will improve the bracketed part of the sentence. In case no improvement is needed, select ‘No improvement’. H̲e̲ ̲s̲p̲e̲n̲t̲ ̲m̲a̲n̲y̲ ̲a̲n̲ ̲h̲o̲u̲r̲s̲ ̲p̲l̲a̲y̲i̲n̲g̲ ̲t̲h̲e̲ ̲c̲h̲e̲s̲s̲ ̲a̲c̲r̲o̲s̲s̲ ̲t̲h̲e̲ city.
options: He had spent many an hours playing the chess across the
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} He have spent many an hours playing the chess across the
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} He spent many an hour playing the chess across the
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The option that will improve the bracketed part of the sentence is: He spent many an hour playing chess across the "He spent many an hour playing chess across the" - This option is the correct improvement because it corrects the errors in the original sentence. "Hour" is the correct singular form. Therefore, the correct option is to choose: "He spent many an hour playing chess across the."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate meaning of the given idiom. Buy a lemon
options: To give importance to little things
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} To buy something that is of much less value.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} To work through the day and shop in the evening
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate meaning of the idiom "Buy a lemon" is: “To buy something that is of much less value.” The idiom "Buy a lemon" means to purchase something, usually a product such as a car, that turns out to be defective or of poor quality, and therefore not worth the money spent on it.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Rearrange the parts of the sentence in correct order. Our study goes P. beyond LDL cholesterol levels to get Q. and the impact of eating walnuts daily R. a complete picture of all of the lipoproteins S. on their potential to improve cardiovascular risk
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct order of the parts of the sentence is: PRQS So, the rearranged sentence is: "Our study goes beyond LDL cholesterol levels to get a complete picture of all of the lipoproteins and the impact of eating walnuts daily on their potential to improve cardiovascular risk."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate one-word substitution for the given group of words. A person who is experiencing dementia brought about by old age
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate one-word substitution for the given group of words is: Senile Senile: Showing signs of old age, especially through mental deterioration; relating to or characteristic of old age. Primal: Relating to the earliest stages in the development of something. Sentinel: A soldier or guard whose job is to stand and keep watch. Primitive: Relating to an early stage in the development of something; basic, rudimentary, or simple.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the word that is opposite in meaning to the given word. Sane
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The word that is opposite in meaning to "Sane" is: Paranoid Sane: Having or showing sound judgment; mentally sound; not insane or mentally disturbed. Paranoid: Exhibiting or characterized by extreme and irrational fear or distrust of others, often to the point of delusion. Reposeful: Characterized by calmness, tranquility, or relaxation; peaceful and undisturbed. Halcyon: Denoting a period of time in the past that was idyllically happy and peaceful. Stoical: Enduring pain, hardship, or adversity without showing or expressing emotions or complaints; characterized by indifference to pleasure or pain.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the word that is opposite in meaning to the given word. Desiccate
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The word that is opposite in meaning to "Desiccate" is: Hydrate Desiccate: To remove moisture from something; to dry thoroughly; to become dried out. Venerate: To regard with great respect or reverence; to honor or worship. Hydrate: To provide or restore water or moisture to something; to make or become hydrated. Ascend: To go up or climb; to rise or move upward in position or rank. Honour: Adherence to what is right or to a conventional standard of conduct; a privilege.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate meaning of the given idiom. Be my guest
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate meaning of the idiom "Be my guest" is: Please do it The idiom "Be my guest" is a polite way of giving someone permission to do something, often with the implication that the speaker is willing and happy to allow it.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the option that is the correct indirect form of the sentence. She said, “I will be available tomorrow.”
options: She said that she must be available the next day.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} She said that she would be available tomorrow.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} She said that she will be available the next day.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} She said that she would be available the next day.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is d: “She said that she would be available the next day.” Direct speech ---- Indirect speech (Change of modals) Shall/will ---- Should/would May ---- Might Can ---- Could Therefore, the correct answer is: “She said that she would be available the next day.”

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the incorrectly spelt word.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The incorrectly spelled word is: TREBBLE RAPPORT: A close and harmonious relationship in which the people or groups concerned understand each other's feelings or ideas and communicate well. TREBLE: To increase threefold; to triple in size, amount, or extent. TUNNEL: A passage that is dug underground, usually through a hill or under a body of water, for road or rail traffic. SUPPORT: To bear all or part of the weight of; to hold up; to give assistance to someone or something.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate one-word substitution for the given group of words. Dependent on chance or uncertainty
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate one-word substitution for the given group of words "Dependent on chance or uncertainty" is: Precarious Precarious: Not securely held or in position; dangerously likely to fall or collapse. Presumptuous: Failing to observe the limits of what is permitted or appropriate. Presume: To suppose that something is the case on the basis of probability or appearance. Paradox: A seemingly contradictory statement, proposition, or situation that may nonetheless be true.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Some parts of a few sentences have been jumbled up, and labelled A, B, C and D. Select the option that gives the correct sequence in which these parts can be rearranged to form a meaningful and grammatically correct sentence. I didn’t speak to the sage again for many years, A. seer, born on a dark island of a union between an ascetic and a fisher princess B. because of the many hefty books, he’d written. Vyasa the C. though I heard of him from time to time. I learned D. his name: Vyasa the Compendious,
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct sequence in which these parts can be rearranged to form a meaningful and grammatically correct sentence is: CDBA So, the sentence would read: "Though I heard of him from time to time, I didn’t speak to the sage again for many years because of the many hefty books he’d written. I learned his name: Vyasa the Compendious, seer, born on a dark island of a union between an ascetic and a fisher princess."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the word that can substitute the given group of words. A leader who sways his followers by his oratory
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The word that can substitute the given group of words "A leader who sways his followers by his oratory" is: Demagogue Demagogue: A political leader who seeks support by appealing to the desires and prejudices of ordinary people, rather than by using rational argument; someone who gains power by stirring up the emotions and prejudices of the masses. Narcissist: A person who has an excessive interest in or admiration of themselves. Fugitive: A person who is fleeing from custody, persecution, or danger. Impostor: A person who pretends to be someone else in order to deceive others, especially for fraudulent gain.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. I would uncover the story that lay (1) ______ between the lines of the men’s exploits. Better still, I (2) ______ one of them tell it herself, with all her joys and doubts, her struggles and her triumphs, her heartbreaks, her achievements, the unique female way (3) ______ which she sees her world and her place in it. And who could be (4) ______ for this than Panchaali? It is her life, her voice, her questions, and her vision that I invite you (5) ______ 'The Palace of Illusions'. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 1.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 1: Invisible Invisible: Unable to be seen; not visible to the eye. Obvious: Easily perceived or understood; clear, apparent, or self-evident. Visible: Able to be seen; perceptible by the eye or through another sense. Detectable: Able to be perceived or noticed; capable of being detected or identified.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. I would uncover the story that lay (1) ______ between the lines of the men’s exploits. Better still, I (2) ______ one of them tell it herself, with all her joys and doubts, her struggles and her triumphs, her heartbreaks, her achievements, the unique female way (3) ______ which she sees her world and her place in it. And who could be (4) ______ for this than Panchaali? It is her life, her voice, her questions, and her vision that I invite you (5) ______ 'The Palace of Illusions'. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 2.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 2: would have In the context of the sentence, the phrase "Better still, I would have one of them tell it herself" suggests a hypothetical situation or a conditional action. The use of "would have" indicates a past hypothetical action that the speaker would have preferred or considered if certain conditions were met. In this case, it implies that the speaker would have preferred to have one of the men tell the story themselves, but for some reason, it was not possible or did not happen. Therefore, "would have" is the most appropriate option to convey this sense of hypothetical or conditional action in the sentence.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. I would uncover the story that lay (1) ______ between the lines of the men’s exploits. Better still, I (2) ______ one of them tell it herself, with all her joys and doubts, her struggles and her triumphs, her heartbreaks, her achievements, the unique female way (3) ______ which she sees her world and her place in it. And who could be (4) ______ for this than Panchaali? It is her life, her voice, her questions, and her vision that I invite you (5) ______ 'The Palace of Illusions'. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 3.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 3: in The word "in" indicates the manner or context in which she sees her world and her place in it. Therefore, "in" is the most appropriate option to convey this sense of perspective or manner in the sentence. Through: Indicating passage or movement from one side or end to another. Though: Despite the fact that; however; nevertheless; in spite of; notwithstanding. By: Indicating the means or method used to achieve or accomplish something

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. I would uncover the story that lay (1) ______ between the lines of the men’s exploits. Better still, I (2) ______ one of them tell it herself, with all her joys and doubts, her struggles and her triumphs, her heartbreaks, her achievements, the unique female way (3) ______ which she sees her world and her place in it. And who could be (4) ______ for this than Panchaali? It is her life, her voice, her questions, and her vision that I invite you (5) ______ 'The Palace of Illusions'. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 4.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 4: Better suited In the given sentence, the phrase "who could be better suited for this than Panchaali?" suggests that the speaker is considering who would be the most appropriate or fitting choice for the task or role mentioned earlier in the passage. The term "better suited" implies that Panchaali is particularly well-matched or suitable for the task or role being discussed. Therefore, "better suited" is the most appropriate option to convey this sense of suitability or compatibility in the sentence.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. I would uncover the story that lay (1) ______ between the lines of the men’s exploits. Better still, I (2) ______ one of them tell it herself, with all her joys and doubts, her struggles and her triumphs, her heartbreaks, her achievements, the unique female way (3) ______ which she sees her world and her place in it. And who could be (4) ______ for this than Panchaali? It is her life, her voice, her questions, and her vision that I invite you (5) ______ 'The Palace of Illusions'. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 5.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 5: into In the given sentence, the phrase "I invite you into 'The Palace of Illusions'" suggests that the speaker is inviting the reader or listener to enter or become immersed in the world of "The Palace of Illusions," which refers to the story or narrative being discussed. The preposition "into" is used to indicate movement or transition from one place or state to another. In this context, it implies that the speaker is inviting the audience to enter or become a part of the imaginative world or narrative of "The Palace of Illusions." Therefore, "into" is the most appropriate option to convey this sense of invitation or immersion in the sentence.
